title: Budget to fuel another RBA rise
full_text:
Australians hoping for interest rate relief won’t get any comfort from the federal budget, with UBS warning that booming government spending will make another rate rise only more likely.
On paper, the deficit is set to beat December’s mid-year update by up to $20bn over the next two years thanks to higher commodity prices and inflation-boosting tax revenue. But the real story lies beneath the surface, where total government spending across federal and state levels continues to surge at an alarming pace.
“The budget overall remains stimulatory,” UBS chief economist George Tharenou said.
“The main driver of that is spending growth, which has been booming.” Government spending is growing at 6-8 per cent annually, driven largely by public sector wages rising 8 per cent. With the economy already operating above its potential amid low productivity, a stimulatory budget will fuel an inflation fire the Reserve Bank is struggling to extinguish.
Tharenou expects the RBA to raise the cash rate by another 25 basis points to 4.6 per cent from here, most likely in August after RBA governor Michele Bullock signalled a pause.
The cash rate is 4.35 per cent.
Tharenou is more hawkish than most economists, who suspect interest rates have peaked.
“Interest rates are likely to stay higher for longer,” he said. “The more likely timing is still August, but I wouldn’t rule out the next meeting in June, post the budget.” The total government deficit including all states widened sharply to $112bn, or 4 per cent of GDP last financial year. Tharenou estimates it will balloon to $147bn, or 5 per cent of GDP this year.
This matters because Bullock has noted that even small changes in demand put upward pressure on inflation when the economy is running hot.
Bullock has characterised the starting point of the economy as operating above potential. But Tharenou sees headline inflation remaining near 5 per cent by year’s end – well above the RBA’s forecast of 4 per cent and far from the target band – and trimmed mean inflation near 4 per cent.
Adding to inflationary pressures are government wage policies. The Fair Work Commission decision on minimum wages due within weeks will likely deliver at least a 4.25 per cent increase, following the government’s recommendation for a real wage rise.
The biggest uncertainty surrounds expected tax changes. The budget will scrap the 50 per cent capital gains tax discount and wind back negative gearing, at least for future purchases of existing homes. “Clients had been quite complacent on the prospect of material changes in the budget, on the taxes,” Tharenou said.
If realised, it would be a “material downside risk to the outlook for asset prices and credit”.
The wealth effect from property can’t be understated. Household wealth increased by $1.5 trillion last year and sits at 11 times income, among the highest in the world. A reversal would pose significant risks to consumer spending and economic growth.
For stocks, UBS strategist Richard Schellbach sees a silver lining in the tax changes, albeit long term.
“I believe it makes equities a more competitive investment space,” Schellbach said. “It’s because the current tax system has relatively favoured property.” Removing the CGT discount levels the playing field between property and shares, potentially redirecting investment flows towards equities over time.
In the stockmarket, the changes favour income-paying stocks over growth stocks, since dividend taxation remains unchanged while capital gains face higher effective tax rates.
“Income generating equities from a tax point of view become slightly more attractive,” Schellbach said. But the short-term outlook for shares remains subdued, he said, and he has reduced his ASX200 target and expects earnings downgrades to continue through mid-year as higher energy costs bite.
The real estate sector faces particular headwinds. “Sentiment around property in general takes a little bit of a hit,” Schellbach said, recommending investors remain underweight in the sector for at least three to six months.
The wildcard is whether any negative gearing changes incentivise new-home construction by exempting new builds. This could eventually boost housing supply, though Tharenou said he doubted builders had the capacity to respond quickly, given existing constraints and cost pressures.
What emerges is a picture of conflicting forces: a federal budget that looks healthier on paper but masks underlying spending pressures, tax changes that could dampen the property market but take years to shift housing supply, and an RBA that finds itself with little choice but to keep raising rates despite concerns about economic risks. The optics may be good but for households already squeezed by the highest interest rates in over a decade, the budget may actually bring more pain than relief.
summary: The upcoming federal budget is expected to exacerbate inflation and lead to another interest rate hike by the RBA, driven by increased government spending and potential tax changes.

heading: Banking and Economy
key_points: Government spending is growing at 6-8% annually, primarily due to rising public sector wages.
UBS predicts the RBA will raise the cash rate by 25 basis points to 4.6% by August.
Proposed tax changes may negatively impact the property market and consumer spending.
relevance: Understanding the implications of government fiscal policy and interest rate movements is crucial for actuaries and financial services professionals in managing risk and advising clients.
